title: .gitignore — What Not to Track
subtitle: Keep sensitive and generated files out of git

sections.content:
Not everything in your project should be tracked by git. Two categories of files should always be excluded:

**Generated files** like `node_modules/` — these can be recreated with a single command (`npm install`). They're huge, change constantly, and would bloat your repository.

**Secret files** like `.env` — these contain passwords, API keys, and other sensitive data. If you commit them to git and push to GitHub, anyone can see your secrets.

The `.gitignore` file tells git: "Pretend these files don't exist. Don't track them, don't commit them, don't even mention them."
keyPoints: node_modules/ is generated — exclude it from git
.env files contain secrets — never commit them
.gitignore tells git which files to ignore
This is one of the first files you should create in any project

sections.instruction: Create a `.gitignore` file and add `node_modules/` as the first entry. The `>` operator creates the file (or overwrites it if it exists).
Now add `.env` to the ignore list. The `>>` operator *appends* to the file instead of overwriting it — an important difference!

troubleshooting.solution: That's okay — it just means node_modules/ was overwritten. Run: echo "node_modules/" > .gitignore && echo ".env" >> .gitignore to fix it.

sections.question: Why should node_modules NOT be committed to git?
options: It's too large and can be regenerated from package.json
sections.explanation: The node_modules folder can contain thousands of files and hundreds of megabytes. Since it can be fully recreated by running `npm install` (which reads package.json), there's no reason to commit it. Keeping it out of git keeps your repository small and clean.

sections.prompt: The file that tells git which files to ignore is called _____.

sections.instruction: Verify your .gitignore is set up correctly. You can run `cat .gitignore` to check its contents.

items.hint: Run: cat .gitignore — you should see node_modules/ on the first line
Run: cat .gitignore — you should see .env on the second line

items.text: You understand why these files shouldn't be committed
items.hint: node_modules is too large and can be recreated. .env contains secrets like passwords and API keys.

completionMessage: Your project is properly configured! Generated files and secrets will stay out of version control. Now for the grand finale — let's build an actual web server.
